source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: New fossil evidence demonstrates that early hominins did not all move bipedally in the same way
Both P. robustus and Australopithecus share external morphological adaptations to terrestrial bipedalism
Internal bone structure shows distinct patterns between the two taxa across the whole lower limb
P. robustus internal bone structure similarities with African apes indicate routine flexion and abduction
Trabecular patterns reflect dorsi- and plantarflexed ankle joint postures combined with flexed hip
These patterns suggest P. robustus may have engaged frequently in vertical climbing behaviors
Geologically younger P. robustus likely used arboreality more frequently than older Sterkfontein Australopithecus

typical_pattern: State broad context or recent advances in the field
Present specific findings from current analysis
Link findings to biomechanical interpretation grounded in comparative anatomy
Defend primary interpretation against alternatives using mechanistic reasoning
Conclude with broader implications for hominin ecology or evolution

likely_priorities: Biomechanical grounding of trabecular bone interpretations in extant primate kinematics
Exclusion of alternative behavioral explanations for observed bone structure patterns
Methodological rigor in distinguishing functional signals from phylogenetic or allometric effects
Adequacy of comparative sample sizes and taxonomic coverage
Integration of internal bone structure with external morphological evidence
likely_attacks: Insufficient kinematic data on wild great apes to support one-to-one correspondence claims
Overlapping functional demands across different behaviors could produce similar trabecular distributions
BHBK terrestrial bipedal gait or prolonged squatting could generate similar loading patterns
Methodological constraints limit ability to move beyond density distributions to orientation analyses
Static loading during sustained postures does not stimulate bone modeling or remodeling
Need for three-dimensional analyses of principal trabecular orientation and micro-finite element analyses
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion explicitly acknowledges that quantitative data on ankle, knee, and hip joint kinematics across different locomotor behaviors in wild great apes remain scarce reducing our ability to make direct comparisons with extant taxa. The authors systematically consider alternative interpretations including terrestrial quadrupedalism, BHBK gait, and squatting, defending against each. They acknowledge methodological constraints limit our ability to move beyond describing density distributions and call for ongoing development of three-dimensional analyses of principal trabecular orientation, micro-finite element analyses, additional kinematic information, and more biomechanically grounded experimental datasets. The phrase a strict one-to-one correspondence between trabecular structure and specific activities could be provided signals awareness that current evidence does not fully establish unique activity signatures.

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile accurately reflects the paper's cautious but assertive tone, its systematic consideration of alternative interpretations, and its acknowledgment of methodological limitations while maintaining clear claims about distinct locomotor repertoires.

_phrase_violations.phrase: reinforcing the functional signal identified in more recent morphological analyses
